title: Leverage Defaults

content.question: What's one default you could engineer this week that would save 10 decisions a day?
content.business_lens: Default onboarding = retention. Default pricing = revenue.
content.life_lens: Default meals, routines, cleaning = your life.
sop: Write out what your ideal day or week looks like if you made zero active decisions.
Rate the quality of your current Default, 1-10, for one key area such as meals, schedule, or follow-ups (see calculation, page 6).
Automate or pre-decide that one area with a subscription, template, standing appointment, or auto-rule.
Remove the manual decision entirely, don't just make it easier, eliminate it.
Let the default run untouched for a month before evaluating whether to adjust it.
questions: What's one default you could engineer this week that would save 10 decisions a day?
How many of today's decisions were things you'd already decided before, but re-decided anyway out of habit?
If you disappeared for two weeks, what would keep running fine on autopilot, and what would immediately fall apart?

definitions.def: The behavior that happens when no decision is made.

content.equation_example: Default quality rated 8 out of 10, running for Time 12 weeks. Outcome equals 8 times 12 = 96, an index, not a fixed unit. Higher means a good default compounds into a strong outcome the longer it runs hands-off; a low Default score means every week that passes on autopilot is quietly working against you.
content.closing_statement: What happens automatically matters more than what you choose.

caption:
Principle Nº 114: Leverage Defaults

Default behavior beats chosen behavior.

Ask yourself: What's one default you could engineer this week that would save 10 decisions a day?
